figures.caption: Table 6: Comparison of Majority Vote performance (maj@16) on the MATH dataset and out-of-domain benchmarks. Results represent the accuracy when selecting the most consistent answer from 16 sampled paths. ∆ values indicate the improvement of Mixed-CUTS over the GRPO baseline.

Figure 1: The Mixed-CUTS Framework. The framework combines exploitative rollouts (Gstd) and exploratory rollouts (GCUTS) to preserve advantage variance under saturated training conditions. The CUTS operator enforces uniform sampling within a constrained Top-K candidate set, decoupling generation from model bias.
Table 3: Key hyperparameters used in MIXED-CUTS.
Table 1: Main results comparing MIXED-CUTS and GRPO on MATH. We report Pass@1 and Pass@16 (%) across five benchmarks, including "Thinking Mode" baselines for reference. ∆ denotes the gain over GRPO.

Figure 2: Training Dynamics (Qwen3-4B). Evolution of (Left) Response Length, (Middle-Left) Policy Entropy, (Middle-Right) AIME25 Reward, and (Right) AIME25 maj@16 consistency. Unlike GRPO (Grey), MIXED-CUTS (Orange) breaks the saturation trap by sustaining high entropy and inducing longer reasoning chains, driving both superior out-of-domain generalization and substantially stronger majority-vote consistency.
